body:

In 1894, archaeôÙolôÙoôÙgist ûdouard Piette disôÙcovôÙered the ãVenus of BrassemôÙpouy,ã othôÙerôÙwise known as the ãLady with the Hood.ã Unearthed in southôÙwestôÙern France and datôÙing to around 25,000ô BCE, this carvôÙing repôÙreôÙsents the earôÙliôÙest realôÙisôÙtic depicôÙtion of a human face. The figureãs foreôÙhead, nose, and brows are careôÙfulôÙly carved in relief, as is the hair, arranged in a neat geoôÙmetôÙric patôÙtern. But what hapôÙpened to the mouth? Or the eyes? Weãre not sure.
The Venus is carved from mamôÙmoth ivory, likeôÙly using a stone flint, and stands just 3.65ô cm tall. For some, it marks a major develôÙopôÙment in figôÙuôÙraôÙtive art. Or, as hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan Simon Schama has sugôÙgestôÙed, this figôÙurine may well be the ãdawn of the idea of beauôÙtyã in human culôÙture.

title: The Greatest Double Agent Ever: How a Spanish Chicken Farmer Became the Most Important Double Agent in WWII
description:

Juan Pujol GarôÙcûÙa was one of the rare indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals whose parôÙticôÙiôÙpaôÙtion in World War II m
body:
in History |   	March 2nd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Juan Pujol GarôÙcûÙa was one of the rare indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals whose parôÙticôÙiôÙpaôÙtion in World War II made him a MemôÙber of the Order of the British Empire and earned him the Iron Cross. He gained that unlikeôÙly disôÙtincôÙtion in perôÙhaps the riskiôÙest of all roles in espiôÙonage, that of a douôÙble agent. Despite ultiôÙmateôÙly workôÙing for the Allied cause, he creôÙatôÙed an elabôÙoôÙrate ficôÙtionôÙal perôÙsona ã comôÙplete with an inventôÙed spy netôÙwork operôÙatôÙing across Great Britain ã who proôÙfessed loyôÙalôÙty to the Nazi cause. Not only did Pujol get this charôÙacôÙter plugged into the real GerôÙman intelôÙliôÙgence sysôÙtem, he also got him on its payôÙroll, receivôÙing what came to the equivôÙaôÙlent of more than $6 milôÙlion in todayãs U.S. dolôÙlars for supôÙplyôÙing inforôÙmaôÙtion ã inforôÙmaôÙtion that ultiôÙmateôÙly conôÙtributed to the Axisã loss of the war.
The stoôÙry of how this chickôÙen farmer from Barcelona became the most imporôÙtant douôÙble agent of World War II is told in the aniôÙmatôÙed PriôÙmal Space video above. Unlike many of the spies hisôÙtoôÙry has rememôÙbered more clearôÙly, Pujol didôÙnãt begin his espiôÙonage career in the employ of any govôÙernôÙment in parôÙticôÙuôÙlar.
RadôÙiôÙcalôÙized, if that be the word, by the expeôÙriôÙence of havôÙing been draftôÙed into the SpanôÙish CivôÙil War, he vowed to dedôÙiôÙcate his life to ãthe good of humanôÙiôÙty.ã Turned away by the British embassy, to which heãd offered his serôÙvices because Britain opposed Nazi GerôÙmany, he went freeôÙlance, re-inventôÙing himôÙself as a Third Reich-loyôÙal SpanôÙish milôÙiôÙtary man seekôÙing an assignôÙment in the U.K. TakôÙen on by GerôÙmany, he instead decamped to LisôÙbon, where he began manôÙuôÙfacôÙturôÙing ersatz intelôÙliôÙgence reports using newsôÙreel footage and tourist brochures.
HowôÙevôÙer makeshift, Pujolãs craft proved impresôÙsive to both GerôÙmany and Britain, which launched an interôÙnaôÙtionôÙal spy hunt for him. He thus accomôÙplished his goal of becomôÙing an offiôÙcial British douôÙble agent, in which capacôÙiôÙty he arrived at his finest hour: misôÙleadôÙing the GerôÙmans as to the 1944 ãDãDayã invaôÙsion of NorôÙmandy in an effort called OperôÙaôÙtion ForôÙtiôÙtude. In SpanôÙish, that would be ForôÙtôÙaleza, which became the title of an RTVE docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary about Pujolãs long-untold stoôÙry a few years ago. But if any sinôÙgle word reflects Pujolãs conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtion to hisôÙtoôÙry, that word must be GarôÙbo, the code name assigned him by his first British case offiôÙcer. After all, what othôÙer name ã at least in 1942 ã could quite so evocaôÙtiveôÙly befit an agent whose skills of craftôÙing and inhabôÙitôÙing inventôÙed charôÙacôÙters made his hanôÙdlers regard him as ãthe best actor in the worldã?

title: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks
description: For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photograp
body:
For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photography, and journaling. Whatãs been missing from these hobbies is something genuinely new, a reason to believe paper can do more than it did before. Radioposter is betting it can.ô 
The Midwest-based startup has built what it calls Paper-fi: physical books with synchronized audio soundtracks that follow readers in real time as they turn each page. No chips embedded in the paper, no QR codes to scan. The system uses patented computer vision and other modes through a smartphone or smart glasses to track your place in the book and play the corresponding audio. Whether music, ambient sound, or narration, the soundtrack moves with you at your own pace. The books themselves are designed more like art objects than traditional reading material, showcasing highly visual stories where audio and imagery carry the narrative together. Think coffee table book meets documentary or movie score.ô 

Sound design, composition, illustration, photography, and storytelling all collapse into a single physical object. Itãs not an audiobook. Itãs not a picture book with a playlist. Itãs a format that didnãt exist before, one in which image, sequence, and sound interact on paper in ways that screens have made us forget.
After years of infinite scroll and algorithmically generated content, people are looking for more tangible hobbies. The appetite for objects you can hold, experiences that have edges and endings, is real. Radioposter is positioning Paper-fi as a way to give the medium new superpowers, making analog competitive again without abandoning what makes internet storytelling compelling.
The company publishes its own titles and the app that makes it all work. Two books have launched already, with more in development. They are also actively looking for artists who want to pitch their work for the format and have programs to support publishing end-to-end.
Whether Paper-fi becomes a lasting medium or a beautiful experiment, it represents something rare: a new analog artifact built for this moment, not borrowed from the past.
Learn more at radioposter.com.

schema.headline: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks

title: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture
description: Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, t
body:
Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, the earlier version was easy enough to fake that there were tens of millions of fraudulent copies in circulation. The same goes for paper bank notes, which over the years have been printed with increasingly high-tech features such as holograms, watermarks, and distinctive material blends. More recently, many countries have also implemented plastic coatings.
Banque de France, a central money-producing outfit in Europe, has adopted a technology called EverFit, which includes a polymer coating on cotton-blend notes that increases durability. Every year, up to three billion notes may be printed, but they must adhere to the strictest standards of technical quality. If they donãt, theyãre shredded. One drawback, though, is that theyãre not recyclable. Thatãs where Paris-based furniture design studio Maximum saw a unique opportunity.

Maximumãs unique line of stools, called Billex, repurposes the masses of discarded bills into modern, functional objects. With a cotton substrate and two layers of plastic coating, the tiny fragments can be manipulated with heat and compressed into a hard shape. Not only is the stool a useful and stylish design piece unto itself, produced in a variety of colors, but the Billex concept is also something of a prototype. It can be employed for a wide range of applications, as the shredded notesãand the stools themselvesãcan be upcycled and compressed into virtually any shape.
See more on Maximumãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture

title: Tshepiso Moropa Splices Setswana Folklore and Dreams into Archival Collages
description: Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we te
body:
Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we tell. The self-taught artist draws on her background in psychology and linguistics as she plumbs African archives and oral histories, reinterpreting her findings through minimal, yet weighty compositions.
Moropa often grounds her works within dinaane and ditoro, Setswana lore and dreams, respectively. ãEach folktale carries a unique blend of history, cultural values, and human experience, serving as a wellspring of inspiration,ã she says in a statement. ãMy work is informed by the timeless wisdom, moral lessons, and magical elements found within Sestwana stories.ã
ãThe Joyã (2023), digital collage on archival matte, 42 x 29.7 centimeters
Fusing digital and analog techniques, the artist assembles surreal scenes with precise lines and ample negative space. Birds figure prominently and serve as spiritual guides as in pieces like ãThe Joy,ã which the artist describes as referencing the following story: ãWhen the girl drifted into the darkness, the ancestors came to her disguised as birds, their wings turning into gentle threads that lifted her away from harm.ã
Other works are more personal, including ãHiding in Plain Sight.ã Depicting an older woman floating above a wooden home, the piece alludes to one of Moropaãs dreams in which her grandmother appeared in the sky, hovering above the artist and her twin sister.
Moropa is based in Johannesburg and represented by THK Gallery in Cape Town. This April, her work will go on view in a group exhibition at Museum Rietberg in Zurich, followed by a presentation at the Biel/Bienne Festival of Photography in Switzerland. Keep up with her practice on Instagram.
ãHiding in Plain Sightã (2025, collage matte paper, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãRiding Shotgunã (2025), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãThe Flying Girlã (2023), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ãThe Ride Homeã (2024), Digital print on Hanhemû¥hle Photo Rag, 40 x 40 centimeters

schema.headline: Tshepiso Moropa Splices Setswana Folklore and Dreams into Archival Collages

title: Ruby Silvious' Tiny Paintings and Assemblages Reimagine Used Teabags
description: From diminutive pieces of fine mesh, Ruby Silvious meticulously conjoins, stains, tears, and sculpts a range mixed-media collages and tiny paintin
body:
From diminutive pieces of fine mesh, Ruby Silvious meticulously conjoins, stains, tears, and sculpts a range mixed-media collages and tiny paintings from repurposed teabags. She often incorporates other elements, such as sardine tins, thread spools, and cut paper elements. The bags are emptied and flattened and sometimes connected to form longer surfaces, often keeping the strings and tabs attached. Other times, Silvious further manipulates the soft paper substrate, creating life-size garments that possess an ethereal, even spectral quality.
This spring, Silviousã work can be seen in group shows in Vermont, Maryland, Philadelphia, Massachusetts, and New York. During the summer, she will be collaborating with MûÀlaga-based artist David Seaton, whose abstract paintings inspired some of Silviousã recent paper pieces, followed by a show of both of their work in Rhonda, Spain. And in the fall, her pieces will also be included in a group exhibition at Gallery of Hermosa. See more and follow updates on Instagram.

schema.headline: Ruby Silvious' Tiny Paintings and Assemblages Reimagine Used Teabags

title: Dive into Wool Creature Lab's World of Vibrant Felted Nudibranchs
description: Among the myriad delights of the marine world, nudibranchs count among some of the most adorable. There are around 3,000 known species of these of
body:
Among the myriad delights of the marine world, nudibranchs count among some of the most adorable. There are around 3,000 known species of these often very colorful, textured, soft-bodied animals. Technically part of the mollusc family, they shed their shells as they grow older, so we sometimes refer to them as ãsea slugs,ã but the name doesnãt exactly live up to their inherent style. For artist Arino Borevich of Wool Creature Lab, however, these unique minuscule beings truly shine in vibrant, felted fiber.
A decade ago, Borevich was working as a cook at a remote biology research station in northern Russiaãs White Sea. ãI was surrounded by 200 marine biologists and students living and working together on a small island,ã she tells Colossal. ãThat summer changed everything. It was there that I first learned about nudibranchsãthese impossibly colorful sea slugs with shapes and patterns that looked like they came from another planet.ã

Wool Creature Lab was born when, as a way to pass time and explore her creativity, Borevich felted a few nudibranchs to gift to some of the scientists who were studying them. She started an Etsy shop and Instagram, going with a gut feeling that these little woolen specimens ãwanted to exist in the world,ã she says. Soon, marine biologists and divers from all over the world began to find her work, some of whom requested particular species, including scientists who had documented certain nudibranchs for the first time.
So far, Borevich has recreated more than 40 different kinds, carefully reimagining real, scientifically described creatures into meticulously crafted fiber versions. She has an ambitious dream to sculpt every one of the thousands of known nudibranchs. ãEach sculpture takes six to 12 hours to make, so this might take a while,ã she says.
More than two dozen of Borevichãs pieces were on view recently during the Xiamen International Art Festival in China as part of the Ark Farm eco-art exhibition. Support her work on Patreon, where she gives away a handmade nudibranch each month.

schema.headline: Dive into Wool Creature Lab's World of Vibrant Felted Nudibranchs
